title: The Switch Statement 🔀
description: A cleaner, safer way to handle multiple decision branches.

slides.title: Too Many If's 🤔
slides.content:
# The problem with 'else if'

If you have 10 possible options (like checking numbers 1 through 10), writing 10 `else if` statements gets incredibly messy.

Swift gives us a beautiful alternative: the **switch statement**.

A switch looks at a single value, and then tries to match it against multiple **cases**.

slides.title: Switch Syntax
slides.content:
Here is what a basic switch statement looks like:

```swift
let medal = "Gold"

switch medal {
case "Gold":
    print("You are 1st! 🥇")
case "Silver":
    print("You are 2nd! 🥈")
case "Bronze":
    print("You are 3rd! 🥉")
default:
    print("You get a participation trophy! 🏆")
}
```

quizData.question: In a switch statement, what does the 'default' case do?

options.text: It acts as a catch-all if none of the specific cases matched.

quizData.explanation: The `default` block is like the final `else` in an `if` chain. It catches everything that fell completely through.

slides.title: Exhaustiveness 🛑
slides.content:
# Switches MUST be Exhaustive

Swift is a safe language. It forces your switch statements to handle **every single possible value**.

If you are switching on an Integer, there are billions of possible Integers. You can't write a case for all of them! Therefore, you **must** include a `default` case to handle the unexpected ones, otherwise your code will refuse to compile.

slides.title: Power Moves 💥
slides.content:
# Grouping and Ranges

Switch cases in Swift are extremely powerful. You can match multiple values using a comma, or match an entire range using `...`!

```swift
let age = 15

switch age {
case 0...12:
    print("You are a child.")
case 13...19:
    print("You are a teenager.")
case 21, 30, 40, 50:
    print("Milestone birthday!")
default:
    print("Just another year.")
}
```

quizData.question: How do you check a range of numbers from 1 to 5 inside a switch case?
quizData.explanation: Swift uses three dots `...` to define a closed range (inclusive of both start and end).
